Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted or just feel too anxious to start. Most of my actual studying happened in bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because I was worried about falling behind. I have all these resources available to me, but organizing myself to use them consistently felt overwhelming. I'd end up listening to recorded lectures while doing other things, which kind of worked for me since I learn better by hearing things anyway.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was about failing. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then suddenly I'd realize a deadline was approaching and panic-study for days. My extracurriculars also made things unpredictable because I'd have commitments that ate into my planned study time. I kept meaning to create a regular schedule, but the anxiety of looking at everything I needed to do made me avoid planning altogether.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd get that sinking feeling that I was missing something important or that everyone else was ahead of me. Sometimes a peer from my support group would message me asking about an assignment, and that would remind me to check. Occasionally I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements, but then I'd get overwhelmed by all the unread content and log out quickly.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine—it always felt like this stressful thing I had to force myself to face. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to check it every morning, but that fell apart quickly when my anxiety spiked. The peer support intervention helped a bit because my study buddy would remind me to check things, but it never became automatic for me.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded sessions were genuinely helpful because I could listen to them while walking or lying down when I felt too anxious to sit at a desk. Discussion boards were surprisingly useful too because I could see how other students understood things, which helped when I was confused but too scared to ask directly. The peer interaction features helped me feel less alone, even if I didn't always participate actively.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just sat there making me feel guilty. I'd open them sometimes, see walls of text, and close them immediately feeling overwhelmed. The optional practice quizzes I avoided because I was scared of seeing how much I didn't know. It's not that I didn't think they were valuable—I just couldn't bring myself to engage with them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I needed to submit or if I could do it passively, like listening. If I could see immediate relevance to an upcoming deadline, I'd push through the anxiety to complete it. Activities that felt collaborative or involved my peers also seemed more worthwhile because the social element made them less terrifying to approach alone.
Q8: Required activities carried this heavy weight of obligation that made them both more important and more anxiety-inducing. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally—like, I was barely keeping up with what was mandatory, so why would I add more? But there was also less pressure with optional stuff, so occasionally I'd engage with something optional when I needed a break from the required work that was stressing me out.
Q9: Yes, constantly. I'd download the audio lectures and listen to them on my phone during commutes or when I couldn't sleep. I also used external YouTube videos to understand concepts because sometimes hearing a different voice explain things helped. My peer support buddy and I would study together in person sometimes, just talking through material without any devices open. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were so many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while doing chores, really absorbing it. Or I'd have long conversations with friends from the peer support group about course concepts over coffee. Sometimes I'd just lie in bed mentally reviewing what I'd learned, trying to calm my anxiety by proving to myself I actually knew things.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture once to download it, and then that single access would turn into hours of offline listening over the next few days. Same with any PDFs I actually did engage with—I'd save them and read them on my tablet later when I felt calmer. The LMS probably thought I barely looked at things, but I was often revisiting downloaded materials repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when my anxiety peaked. Around midterms, I felt completely paralyzed—I knew I needed to study but I was so scared of confirming I didn't understand things that I just avoided everything. My attendance dropped because showing up felt terrifying. The peer support helped pull me back a bit, but there were weeks where engaging felt physically impossible.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from anxiety or overwhelmed by my extracurriculars, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. The workload felt insurmountable even when it probably wasn't that bad objectively. Confusion made it worse because I'd log in, not understand something, feel stupid and scared, and then log out without accomplishing anything. It was a vicious cycle.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while I was too anxious to actually focus, just so I could tell myself I'd watched them. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd stare at assignment instructions for twenty minutes, reading the same sentences over and over because my brain was too clouded with worry to process anything.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially anything that felt evaluative. Starting assignments was the hardest because it meant confronting whether I actually understood the material. I knew they were important—that's exactly why they scared me so much. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel just as anxious. The fear of failing made me avoid the very things that could help me succeed.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to engage. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then a deadline would loom and I'd frantically catch up on everything. It wasn't healthy—I'd pull all-nighters fueled by panic, which made my anxiety worse afterward. But without that external pressure, I probably would have engaged even less. Deadlines were terrifying but necessary.
Q17: Feedback affected me intensely, maybe too much. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would send me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I finally looked and it wasn't terrible, I'd feel relieved enough to engage a bit more.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my anxiety episodes, bad because there wasn't enough external structure to keep me on track. The peer support intervention helped because it added some accountability. But overall, I needed more scaffolding than the course naturally provided.
Q19: Having audio options made engagement easier since that matches how I learn best. The discussion forums were designed well and felt less intimidating than speaking up in person. But the sheer volume of content made engagement harder—seeing everything I needed to do listed out triggered my anxiety. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, checking things compulsively but being too overwhelmed to actually complete work. Or someone who's exploring content trying to understand it but struggling to translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious clicking.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My attendance and login frequency were low, but I was studying offline a lot and still managed decent grades. Some people just don't need to be constantly visible in the system to be learning. Efficiency matters too—maybe they're focused learners who get what they need quickly and then study independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in or how long they spend on videos. But they completely miss the emotional experience—whether someone was actually absorbing content or just going through the motions while panicking. They miss offline study, peer conversations, and all the mental processing that happens away from screens. They show behavior but not understanding or wellbeing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and making genuine effort to understand it, even when it's hard. For me, engagement isn't just about logging in—it's about whether I'm mentally present and trying. Sometimes I'm engaged while lying in bed thinking about concepts, and sometimes I'm completely disengaged while staring at the LMS. It's internal, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much anxiety shaped every interaction I had with the course. It can't show the hours I spent studying offline or the support I got from peers outside the platform. It doesn't capture that my low attendance wasn't apathy—it was fear. And it definitely doesn't show how the peer support intervention genuinely helped me feel less alone, even if my login numbers stayed low.
